metadata.title: Why a live bot hands pairing codes to strangers, then suddenly stops
metadata.topic: Access control via pairing state machine
metadata.purpose: A two-phase state machine solves a bootstrapping problem — the bot can't know your ID without talking to you, but can't restrict to your ID without knowing it. Pairing mode captures your ID via a code only you could execute; then it flips to allowlist mode. The code is proof-of-access.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: adding users after pairing; the pairing-code generation algorithm
metadata.visual_object: A state machine with two circles (pairing-mode, allowlist-mode), an arc labeled `pair <code>`, and each circle's rule beside it.
metadata.hook: A brand-new bot is live and receiving DMs from strangers, but the security mechanism is to send yourself a pairing code first.

beats.narration_text: Discord bot launches. Strangers DM it; the bot replies with a 6-character code. You DM your own bot, get `ABC123`, run `/discord:access pair ABC123` in Claude. The bot switches to allowlist mode and stops accepting pairing codes entirely.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Access control usually whitelists trusted users up front, so why does this bot start by handing pairing codes to everyone, then flip to rejecting all unknowns?
A brand-new bot is live and receiving DMs from strangers, but the security mechanism is to send yourself a pairing code first.

A two-phase state machine solves a bootstrapping problem — the bot can't know your ID without talking to you, but can't restrict to your ID without knowing it. Pairing mode captures your ID via a code only you could execute; then it flips to allowlist mode. The code is proof-of-access.
beats.visual: A state machine with two circles (pairing-mode, allowlist-mode), an arc labeled `pair <code>`, and each circle's rule beside it.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: You deploy a personal relay. Policy is `pairing`. A coworker texts it → reply `pairing-code: X1Y2Z3`. You text it from your phone → `pairing-code: A9B8C7`. You run `pair A9B8C7`. Next coworker message is silently rejected.

A two-phase state machine solves a bootstrapping problem — the bot can't know your ID without talking to you, but can't restrict to your ID without knowing it. Pairing mode captures your ID via a code only you could execute; then it flips to allowlist mode. The code is proof-of-access.
